behaviors.description: Request with mismatched HMAC-SHA256 signature is rejected with 401 response and body is not forwarded to the queue.
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is a deterministic cryptographic function (HMAC-SHA256 always produces the same output for the same inputs). The real failure boundary is whether the handler correctly calls crypto.verify with the raw body and secret. A Unit test using real crypto.verify can catch this by sending requests with valid and invalid signatures and verifying the response status and queue state. The key risk—body modification before validation—is testable in-process without I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates requests with both valid and invalid HMAC-SHA256 signatures (computed on the raw body). Send invalid-signature request, verify 401 response and queue remains empty. Send valid-signature request, verify 200 and event is queued. Use actual crypto.verify rather than mocks to catch body-handling issues.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test can run entirely in-process with real crypto primitives; no external I/O or concurrency needed.

behaviors.description: The event is persisted to the queue and acknowledged before the HTTP 200 response is sent.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on async ordering between queue persistence and response transmission. A Unit test with a mocked queue can verify that the code *intends* to await queue.write before sending response, but cannot detect if the code actually omits the await. The real failure—response sent before queue.write completes, causing event loss—requires testing the ordering semantics of actual queue I/O. A mock queue that returns instantly is fundamentally different from a queue that takes time to persist, potentially hiding the failure if async handling is broken.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test using a real queue (or queue with realistic async semantics). Send a valid request and intercept both the queue write completion and HTTP response timing. Instrument or spy on the queue to verify write() completes before the 200 status code is sent back to the client. Can use in-memory queue with actual async I/O semantics.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires crossing the boundary between handler logic and persistent storage; in-process mock may hide async bugs.

behaviors.description: The HTTP 200 response is sent to the client without waiting for downstream event processing to complete.
behaviors.justification: This is pure in-process control flow: verify that the response is sent before awaiting downstream processing. The failure mode (response blocked on slow downstream) is easily testable with a mock downstream processor that returns a never-resolving Promise. No I/O boundaries crossed; all code paths execute within the handler's process. A Unit test with a stubborn mock (one that never settles) directly exposes if the code incorrectly blocks on downstream.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that mocks the downstream processor to return an unresolved Promise (e.g., new Promise(() => {})). Send a valid request and verify the HTTP 200 response is sent and received before the downstream Promise resolves. Use a timeout or immediate assertion to show the response completes in milliseconds while downstream hangs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure in-process logic; no need for real I/O or concurrency.

behaviors.description: HTTP response is delivered within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when multiple concurrent requests are in flight and downstream processing of prior events is slow.
behaviors.justification: Real failures here are event-loop stalls, OS scheduling delays, and I/O contention under concurrent load—none of which can be faithfully simulated with mocks. A Unit test with mocked slow operations runs on a single thread with predictable timing; it won't expose the actual race conditions and event-loop delays that occur with real concurrency and I/O. Integration-level testing with in-process concurrency could partially address this, but true 3-second wall-clock SLA validation requires a real HTTP server under concurrent load, potentially with real downstream calls or realistic network I/O delays that measure actual system behavior.
behaviors.test_description: System test: start a real HTTP server instance. Send 15+ concurrent requests (using Promise.all or similar), each with a mocked downstream that sleeps for 2+ seconds. Measure wall-clock response arrival time for each request from first to last. Assert all responses arrive within 3-second SLA despite slow downstream processing. Repeat test multiple times to catch timing race conditions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real HTTP server, concurrent requests, and wall-clock timing measurement; in-process mocks cannot faithfully reproduce system-level timing behavior.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time (deduplication).
behaviors.justification: Deduplication requires checking and updating shared state atomically. A Unit test with sequential duplicate events and a mocked ID cache can verify the happy path, but the real failure boundary is race conditions when two requests with the same event ID arrive concurrently. A mock ID cache won't expose synchronization bugs (missing locks, non-atomic check-then-write, etc.). The failure—two queue writes instead of one—only manifests under concurrent load with realistic shared state. Integration testing with concurrent in-process requests hitting real or credible shared state (e.g., in-memory Set or database) is required.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with concurrent duplicate requests. Send two HTTP requests with identical event IDs simultaneously (e.g., using Promise.all or thread-like concurrency model in Node.js). Verify the queue contains exactly one copy of the event, not two. Also run sequential duplicate test to verify basic dedup logic. Check the dedup store (database, in-memory cache) to confirm only one ID record exists.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Race conditions in deduplication logic only exposed under real concurrent access to shared state.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns HTTP 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is error-path handling logic: does the code correctly catch a 'queue full' error and return the right response? A Unit test can mock the queue to throw a 'full' error and verify the handler catches it and responds with 503 and Retry-After header. No real I/O or concurrency needed; this is pure request/response logic that happens in-process. The failure (returning 500 instead of 503, missing header) is easily exposed by a mock.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that configures the mocked queue to raise a 'full' error (or reject the promise) when queue.write() is called. Send a valid request, verify the response is 503 (not 200 or 500) and includes a Retry-After header with a reasonable delay value (e.g., 60 seconds). Test both the error case and a passing case to ensure the handler doesn't break on normal operation.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Error handling logic; mock queue error is sufficient to test the response path.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects inserts that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue implementation itself, not the handler. Verifying that the queue actually enforces capacity requires testing the storage mechanism—inserting N items, checking they persist, attempting N+1, and verifying rejection or eviction. A mock queue could be configured to always return 'full' after N items without actually storing anything. The real failure—queue accepts 1000 items when capacity is 10—only appears by actually reading back what was stored. This is an integration test of the queue, not the handler.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test of the queue in isolation (not the handler). Create a queue instance with a configured capacity (e.g., 10). Write N items and verify all succeed. Attempt to write the (N+1)th item and verify it is rejected or an older item is evicted. Read the queue back and verify it contains exactly N items. This tests the queue's actual storage enforcement, not just its error API.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Tests queue implementation directly; requires actual persistent storage and capacity tracking.
